Off Calendar Jun-10-2021 Continued To Aug-11-21 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO TRANSFER
Off Calendar Jun-28-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. WALT CUBBERLY, Esq. of Williams Hart Boundas Easterby, LLP (Tel: 713-230-2200) for plaintiff Jane Doe;
Atty. RACHEL M. GARRAD, Esq. of Higgs Fletcher & Mack LLP (Tel: 619-236-1551) for cross-defendant Essa Mogammad Arzo;
Atty. ELENI A. SWANK, Esq. of Buchalter (Tel: 949-760-1121) for defendants/cross-complainants Uber Technologies, Inc., Rasier, LLC, and Rasier-CA, LLC.

HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANT ESSA ARZO'S MOTION TO TRANSFER.

Matter argued and submitted.

The Court adopts its tentative ruling but adds "without prejudice," as follows:

Cross-Defendant Essa Mohammad Arzo's motion to transfer venue from San Francisco County to San Diego County is denied without prejudice.

The general venue rule of Code of Civil Procedure section 395(a) provides that an action for personal injuries may be tried "in either the county where the injury occurs . . . or the county where the defendants, or some of them reside at the commencement of the action." When individuals and corporations are joined as defendants, an action brought in the county where the corporation has its principal place of business is the proper county for the action. (Hale v. Bohannon (1952) 38 Cal. 2d 458, 473.) "The individual defendant joined with the corporation may not secure a change of venue in such circumstances because, under section 395, 'some' of the defendants reside in the county where the action was commenced." (Id.)

Here, it is undisputed that Defendant Uber Technologies' principal place of business is in San Francisco. Thus, because "some" of the defendants reside in the county where Plaintiff's action was commenced, San Francisco is a proper venue for this action. (See Delgado v. Superior Court (1977) 74 Cal.App.3d 560, 563 [county in which business entity has its principal place of business is its residence for venue purposes].)

Cross-Defendant also seeks to change venue for the convenience of witnesses under Code of Civil Procedure section 397(c). However, he does not meet his "burden of proving both that the change will be more convenient to the witnesses and that the ends of justice will be best served by the change." (Henson v. Superior Court (1963) 218 Cal. App. 2d 327, 329.) A moving party under section 397(c) must file affidavits that set forth "the names of the witnesses, the nature of the testimony expected from each, and the reasons why the attendance of each would be inconvenient." (Peiser v. Mettler (1958) 50 Cal.2d 594, 607.) Cross-Defendant's bare assertion, in two sentences in her counsel's declaration, that Plaintiff has disclosed in discovery that she sought medical treatment in San Diego County, and has "disclosed witnesses to the events preceding the alleged incident who presumably also reside in around San Diego County" (Spaid Decl., par. 2) is insufficient to meet his burden. (See Dillman v. Superior Court (1972) 205 Cal.App.2d 769, 774 ["There is no showing in this affidavit or in the entire record before us to indicate the nature of the expected testimony of these prospective witnesses, most of whom are unnamed, thus making it impossible for the court to determine whether they are material and necessary witnesses for defendants. Further, it is not stated that the witnesses will be inconvenienced by attending trial in [the forum] County or that the ends of justice will be promoted by a change of venue"].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Eric Throne, CSR No. 7855, Email: eric.throne.csr@gmail.com, Tel: 707-305-8964 (302/EPS)

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-05-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Attys. WALT CUBBERLY, Esq. of Williams Hart Boundas Easterby, LLP (Tel: 713-230-2200) and JESSICA DAYTON, Esq. of ADZ Law, LLP (Tel: 650-458-2300) for plaintiff;
Attys. GARY A. WOLENSKY, Esq. and ROBERT ATKINS, Esq. of Buchalter (Tel: 949-760-1121) for Defendants Uber Technologies, Inc. and Rasier, LLC.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. AND RAISIER, LLC'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Uber Technologies, Inc. and Rasier, LLC's motion to strike is denied in part and granted in part. Plaintiff's "allegations for the purpose of prejudicing Uber" are colorful, but within the realm of acceptable pleading, particularly for background. Because the basis for punitive damages is pled with insufficient particularity, that part of the motion is granted with leave to amend.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-05-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Attys. WALT CUBBERLY, Esq. of Williams Hart Boundas Easterby, LLP (Tel: 713-230-2200) and JESSICA DAYTON, Esq. of ADZ Law, LLP (Tel: 650-458-2300) for plaintiff;
Attys. GARY A. WOLENSKY, Esq. and ROBERT ATKINS, Esq. of Buchalter (Tel: 949-760-1121) for Defendants Uber Technologies, Inc. and Rasier, LLC.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. AND RASIER, LLC'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Uber Technologies, Inc. and Rasier, LLC's demurrers to five of the complaint's numbered causes of action are sustained or overruled as follows:
4 (fraud). The court sustains the demurrer to the fraud cause of action without leave to amend based on claim preclusion. Plaintiff's claim is predicated on Uber's alleged statements regarding "safe rides" and background checks. Those claims were previously asserted and resolved as part of a class action settlement in McKnight v. Uber Technologies, Inc., 4:14-cv-05615-JST (N.D. Cal. Jun. 1, 2017), ECF No. 125. An amended (and final) Stipulation of Settlement (the "Settlement") was reached and entered on June 1, 2017. Id. 4:14-cv-05615-JST (N.D. Cal. Jun. 1, 2017), ECF No. 125. Plaintiff does not address this issue in her opposition and apparently concedes that she was a member of the Class defined in the Settlement and her fraud claim is therefore precluded by the Settlement and the Release. (See Louie v. BFS Retail & Commercial Operations LLC (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 1544, 1555 ["[A] judgment in a class action is binding on class members in any subsequent litigation"]; Villacres v. ABM Indus. Inc. (2010) 189 Cal.App.4th 562, 577 ["Res judicata applies to a court-approved settlement agreement in a class action dismissed with prejudice."].)
5-7 (assault, sexual battery, intentional infliction of emotional distress). Defendants' demurrer ignores the complaint's allegations that "Uber is a common carrier" and thus liable for assaults "regardless of whether such acts were committed within the course and scope of employment for Uber." (Cmpl., 16:15-2.) These allegations may or may not prove factually founded, but that is not for resolution at the pleading state. This demurrer is overruled.
9 (Ralph Act). The complaint adequately pled the elements of a Ralph Act claim as to driver Doe One. (Cmplt., 27:7-28:4; CACI 3063.) Plaintiff's opposition suggests she can also plead theories of Ralph Act liability against the demurring defendants, but has yet to do so. This demurrer is sustained with leave to amend.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)
